Notes on customizing the ‘compute’ scripts¶

When computing statistics using Sempery, whichever type of statistics is being computed, raw data need to be extracted. For most systems, SemperPy is configured to locate and retrieve files containing raw data using the description of the data given by the user as a list of keywords (please see Observation statistic - computing for information about configuring the data retriever).

When running SemperPy with research experiments, it may well be that your files containing raw data are not in a standard location and you will need to tell SemperPy how to find them.

  • your files are in a particular location and you want SemperPy to find them and do its job as a one off operation,
  • your files are named consistently and are perhaps stored in a known directory hierarchy.

SemperPy can cope with both situations, it seems useful to group that information under this title.

Using files in a one off location¶

Computing directives whichever the type of data, are in the form:

compute(
    exvper =
    date =
    type =
    ...
)

If you have files to be used by a compute statement, you can force the compute directive to find the file by specifying different keywords:

obsstat(
    exvper =
    date =
    type =
    ...
    root_tmpl =
    tree_tmpl =
    file_tmpl =
    archive_type =
    )

Those three keyword enable you to define:

  • root_tmpl is the template for the root directory of the data structure
  • tree_tmpl is the directory structure under the root
  • file_tmpl is the template to generate the file name.
  • archive_type defines the type of data storage. The existing types of storage are currently dmf for the dmf archive, in that case it also manages to handle dmget to stage the data first and filesystem indicates the file is available directly. Other types could be created such as ftp or scp.

Let’s go through a few examples:

# in /home/cgibert/obs you can find *diag*.ods (standard naming) files which you would like to use for computing statistics,
# you will need to specify:

root_tmpl = '/home/cgibert/obs',
tree_tmpl = '',
file_tmpl = '<expver>.diag_<collection>.date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods'
archive_type = 'filesystem' # (otherwise SemperPy will try to stage the data)

# in /home/cgibert/obs you can find directories:
# Y2012/M02/D01/H00
# observation files

root_tmpl = '/home/cgibert/obs',
tree_tmpl = Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)/Ddate(@d)/Hdate(@H)
file_tmpl = '<expver>.diag_<collection>.date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods'
archive_type = 'filesystem' # (otherwise SemperPy will try to stage the data)

# in /home/cgibert/obs you can find directories:
# Y2012/M02/D10/H00
# impact files

# operational files (configured like so by default):

root_tmpl = '/home/cgibert/obs',
tree_tmpl = 'Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)/Ddate(@d)/Hdate(@H)'
file_tmpl = '<expver>.imp3_txe_<collection>.obs.date-9(@Y@m@d_@Hz)+date+24(@Y@m@d_@Hz)-date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods'
archive_type = 'filesystem' # (otherwise SemperPy will try to stage the data)

# in a research experiment:

root_tmpl = '/home/cgibert/obs',
tree_tmpl = 'Ydate-9(@Y)/Mdate-9(@m)/Ddate-9(@d)/Hdate-9(@H)',
file_tmpl = '<expver>.imp3_txe_<collection>.obs.date-9(@Y@m@d_@Hz)+date+24(@Y@m@d_@Hz)-date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods'
archive_type = 'filesystem' # (otherwise SemperPy will try to stage the data)

As you can see, files can be described using templates for the root directory, subdirectories and a filename name, these can be customized. This method describes how to instruct SemperPy about the location of data files for one particular compute directive. This can be done in a more permanent way.

Configuring SemperPy for your experiments¶

If all your research experiments are stored following the same storage scheme (which seems to be the case generally), then your file names are the same and the directory structure depends mostly on the experiment id (expver in SemperPy). In that case, you can write a configuration file to describe the location of the file, which will then be used by SemperPy for all your research experiments.

You can customize the configuration of SemperPy (Configuring SemperPy), and particularly the data Retriever (please see Find ODS files).

Choosing a database¶

Whenever you compute statistics to store them in a database, you need to choose a database which will store the data, or you could even create your own database to hold your results (Creating a database). You can read Databases for more general information. For research experiments, there are currently three pre-defined databases:

  • im_exp for observation impact statistics
  • ob_exp for observation statistics
  • fc_exp for forecast skill scores

Alternative storage¶

You can decide to run statistic calculations without storing the results in a database. The keyword storage can be used for that. There are currently three different storers built in SemperPy:

  • dbstorer (storage in a relational database),
  • filestorer (data is saved in a text file which can then be loaded into a database), when filestorer is used, the keyword output_file aliased to database is used to hold the file name,
  • nullstorer (data is not stored), this is useful to test a computing script and make sure everything is ok.

It is possible and relatively easy to write new storers for other needs (e.g. netcdf files, sending data over a network connection, etc...)

    • Configuring SemperPy for your experiments
    • Choosing a database
    • Alternative storage
